one_liner: A newborn boy delivered to a 42-year-old G3P3 mother is noted to have hypotonia, characteristic facial features, and a murmur on routine nursery exam.

stage2.hpi_extension: The infant was born at 38 weeks gestation to a 42-year-old mother who declined first-trimester aneuploidy screening. Delivery was uncomplicated. On exam the baby is markedly hypotonic ('floppy baby') and has feeding difficulty. A holosystolic murmur is heard at the left lower sternal border. He has not yet passed meconium at 30 hours of life and his abdomen is becoming distended, raising concern for duodenal atresia or Hirschsprung disease.
stage2.family_history: Two healthy older siblings; no family history of congenital anomalies
stage2.social_history: Lives with married parents; mother is a teacher
stage2.physical_exam: General: hypotonic newborn. HEENT: flat facial profile, upslanting palpebral fissures, epicanthal folds, small low-set ears, protruding tongue with small mouth, Brushfield spots on irides. Neck: excess nuchal skin. Chest: holosystolic murmur at left lower sternal border (endocardial cushion/AV canal defect). Abdomen: distended; bilious emesis after feeding. Extremities: single transverse palmar crease bilaterally, wide sandal gap between 1st and 2nd toes, short fifth finger with clinodactyly.

lab_results.result: Karyotype: 47,XY,+21 (trisomy 21). FISH confirms three copies of chromosome 21.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Full trisomy 21 (meiotic nondisjunction, ~95% of cases) confirms the diagnosis of Down syndrome. Risk rises sharply with advanced maternal age.

lab_results.result: Abdominal x-ray: 'double bubble' sign (dilated stomach and proximal duodenum with no distal gas) consistent with duodenal atresia. Echocardiogram: complete atrioventricular septal (endocardial cushion) defect.
lab_results.clinical_significance: Duodenal atresia and AV septal (endocardial cushion) defects are both strongly associated with Down syndrome. Endocardial cushion defect is the most common congenital heart lesion in trisomy 21.

stage5.final_clue: Prenatal labs drawn during the mother's second trimester (which she did not review) are retrieved: low AFP, low estriol, high beta-hCG, and high inhibin A — the classic quad screen pattern for Down syndrome.

debrief.classic_presentation: Down syndrome (trisomy 21) is the most common viable autosomal trisomy and the most common genetic cause of intellectual disability. Newborns show hypotonia, flat facies, upslanting palpebral fissures, epicanthal folds, a single transverse palmar crease, clinodactyly of the fifth finger, and a wide sandal gap. Common associated anomalies include endocardial cushion (AV septal) defects, duodenal atresia, Hirschsprung disease, and an increased lifetime risk of acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) and early-onset Alzheimer dementia.
debrief.pathophysiology: About 95% of cases are due to meiotic nondisjunction, most often in maternal meiosis I, producing 47,XX,+21 or 47,XY,+21. Risk rises sharply with advanced maternal age. Approximately 4% result from an unbalanced Robertsonian translocation (often 14;21) and 1% from mosaicism. Triplication of genes on chromosome 21 (including APP) drives the clinical phenotype and explains the near-universal development of Alzheimer-type pathology by the fifth decade.
debrief.treatment_overview: Surgical repair of cardiac defects and duodenal atresia in the neonatal period. Early intervention with physical, occupational, and speech therapy to support development. Routine screening for hypothyroidism, atlantoaxial instability, hearing and vision problems, and leukemia. Lifelong multidisciplinary care; genetic counseling for the family.
high_yield_pearls: Quad screen in Down syndrome: low AFP, low estriol, high beta-hCG, and high inhibin A.
Endocardial cushion (AV septal) defect is the most common congenital heart defect in Down syndrome.
Duodenal atresia ('double bubble' sign) and Hirschsprung disease are classic GI associations.
Increased risk of acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) and AML, plus early-onset Alzheimer dementia (APP gene on chromosome 21).
Advanced maternal age is the major risk factor; most cases result from meiotic nondisjunction in maternal meiosis I.
sources: First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Biochemistry chapter, 'Autosomal trisomies — Down syndrome (trisomy 21)' section.
First Aid for the USMLE Step 1 (2017) — Cardiovascular chapter, 'Congenital heart diseases' section.
